title: Getting started with Ritable
text: Ritable estimates narrow-sense heritability (h²) quantitative traits family cohort studies using profile-likelihood variance-components approach, without requiring SOLAR Eclipse proprietary software. three main steps : Build additive genetic relationship matrix (GRM) pedigree. Estimate heritability one trait herit_vc(). Scale many traits herit_batch(), visualise plot_forest().

title: Getting started with Ritable
text: pedigree data frame needs four columns: individual ID, father ID, mother ID, sex (1 = male, 2 = female). Missing parents coded NA 0. diagonal 1 non-inbred individuals. Full siblings share 0.5 genetic material average, -diagonal entries sibling pairs 0.5.
code: # Minimal example pedigree (founders + one generation of offspring) ped <- data.frame(   id  = 1:8,   pat = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 3, 3),   mom = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 2, 2, 4, 4),   sex = c(1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2) )  # Study subjects are the offspring (IDs 5-8) A <- build_grm(ped, study_ids = 5:8) round(A, 3) #>     5   6   7   8 #> 5 1.0 0.5 0.0 0.0 #> 6 0.5 1.0 0.0 0.0 #> 7 0.0 0.0 1.0 0.5 #> 8 0.0 0.0 0.5 1.0

what: 2. Estimate heritability for a single trait
title: Getting started with Ritable
text: returned list contains:
code: set.seed(42)  # Simulate some phenotype data for the study subjects dat <- data.frame(   IID     = 5:8,   age     = c(35, 38, 40, 44),   sex_num = c(1, 2, 1, 2),   bmi     = c(24.1, 27.3, 22.8, 29.5) )  # Unadjusted model res_unadj <- herit_vc("bmi", grm = A, data = dat, min_n = 3) #> ✔ bmi_unadj  n=4  h2=0.001 [0,1]  p=0.5  # Adjusted model (age + sex) res_adj <- herit_vc("bmi", grm = A, data = dat,                     covs  = c("age", "sex_num"),                     label = "bmi_adj",                     min_n = 3) #> ✔ bmi_adj  n=4  h2=0.001 [NA,NA]  p=0.5  str(res_unadj) #> List of 11 #>  $ label     : chr "bmi_unadj" #>  $ trait     : chr "bmi" #>  $ covariates: chr "" #>  $ n         : int 4 #>  $ h2        : num 0.001 #>  $ se        : num 44.7 #>  $ ci_lo     : num 0 #>  $ ci_hi     : num 1 #>  $ pval      : num 0.5 #>  $ sigma2_a  : num 0.00071 #>  $ sigma2_e  : num 0.712

title: Getting started with Ritable
text: real analyses typically run many traits across several covariate models. herit_batch() handles returns tidy data frame.
code: # Add a second trait dat$hdl <- c(55.0, 60.2, 48.3, 72.1)  covs_list <- list(   unadj = NULL,   cov1  = c("age", "sex_num") )  res <- herit_batch(   traits    = c("bmi", "hdl"),   grm       = A,   data      = dat,   covs_list = covs_list,   min_n     = 3,   .progress = FALSE )  res #>       label trait  covariates n    h2      se ci_lo ci_hi pval sigma2_a #> 1 bmi_unadj   bmi             4 0.001 44.7214     0     1  0.5  0.00071 #> 2 hdl_unadj   hdl             4 0.001 44.7214     0     1  0.5  0.00071 #> 3  bmi_cov1   bmi age+sex_num 4 0.001      NA    NA    NA  0.5  0.00017 #> 4  hdl_cov1   hdl age+sex_num 4 0.001      NA    NA    NA  0.5  0.00017 #>   sigma2_e #> 1  0.71206 #> 2  0.71206 #> 3  0.17143 #> 4  0.17143

title: Getting started with Ritable
code: if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   plot_forest(res,               model_filter  = "cov1",               colour_by     = "trait",               title         = "Adjusted heritability (age + sex)") }

title: Getting started with Ritable
text: typical family cohort workflow looks like :
code: library(Ritable)  # 1. Load pedigree (full pedigree including founders) ped <- read.csv("baependi_pedigree_full.csv")  # 2. Load analysis dataset dat <- read.csv("baependi_analysis_dataset.csv") dat$age2    <- dat$age^2 dat$age_sex <- dat$age * dat$sex_num  # 3. Build GRM once -- reuse for all traits A <- build_grm(ped,                study_ids = dat$IID,                id_col    = "IID",                pat_col   = "pat",                mom_col   = "mat",                sex_col   = "sex_num")  # 4. Define covariate models covs_list <- list(   unadj = NULL,   cov1  = c("age", "sex_num"),   cov2  = c("age", "sex_num", "age2"),   cov3  = c("age", "sex_num", "age2", "age_sex") )  # 5. Run batch estimation brain_traits <- c("k", "S", "I", "AvgThickness", "localGI_corrected")  res <- herit_batch(   traits    = brain_traits,   grm       = A,   data      = dat,   covs_list = covs_list )  # 6. Save and plot write.csv(res, "table_heritability_brain.csv", row.names = FALSE) plot_forest(res, model_filter = "cov2",             title = "Brain morphology heritability (age + sex + age2)")

title: R-itable — Pedigree-based heritability estimation
text: Profile-likelihood heritability estimation family cohort studies — SOLAR required.  ⚠️ Ritable early development formally tested. API may change without notice, estimation results yet validated reference implementation SOLAR Eclipse, package undergone peer review. Use caution verify outputs independently using research context.

title: R-itable — Pedigree-based heritability estimation
text: R-itable (library(Ritable)) estimates narrow-sense heritability (h²) quantitative traits pedigree-based family cohort studies. implements profile-likelihood variance-components approach equivalent SOLAR Eclipse — without proprietary dependencies, compiled code, external binaries. Built neuroimaging biomedical cohorts need run heritability dozens traits across multiple covariate models get results can trace back first principles.

title: R-itable — Pedigree-based heritability estimation
text: 🔬 Profile-likelihood VC estimator — eigendecomposition GRM, 1-D optimisation, exact LRT one-sided χ²(1) boundary correction (matching SOLAR). 📐 Profile-likelihood CIs — Wald ±1.96 SE; proper asymmetric intervals via uniroot(). 🔄 INT transformation — inverse-normal transform applied internally; also exported int_transform() use pipelines. 📦 Batch mode — herit_batch() iterates traits × covariate models returns tidy data frame, ready tables figures. 🌲 Forest plots — plot_forest() immediate visualisation batch output (requires ggplot2). 🧩 Minimal dependencies — core functions require base R, kinship2, rlang, cli.

title: R-itable — Pedigree-based heritability estimation
code: R-itable/ ├── R/ │   ├── itable-package.R       # package-level docs │   ├── build_grm.R            # build additive GRM from pedigree │   ├── int_transform.R        # rank-based inverse-normal transform │   ├── herit_vc.R             # single-trait VC estimator │   ├── herit_batch.R          # batch wrapper │   └── plot_forest.R          # ggplot2 forest plot ├── tests/testthat/ │   ├── helper-fixtures.R      # shared synthetic pedigree/data │   ├── test-build_grm.R │   ├── test-int_transform.R │   ├── test-herit_vc.R │   └── test-herit_batch.R ├── vignettes/ │   └── getting-started.Rmd ├── data-raw/ │   └── prepare_data.R ├── DESCRIPTION ├── NAMESPACE └── NEWS.md

title: R-itable — Pedigree-based heritability estimation
code: install.packages(c("kinship2", "rlang", "cli"))  # For plotting: install.packages("ggplot2")

title: R-itable — Pedigree-based heritability estimation
code: # From GitHub (recommended while pre-CRAN) remotes::install_github("circadia-bio/R-itable")

title: R-itable — Pedigree-based heritability estimation
text: full walkthrough see vignette("getting-started", package = "Ritable").
code: library(Ritable)  # 1. Build GRM from pedigree A <- build_grm(my_pedigree, study_ids = my_data$IID)  # 2. Single trait herit_vc("bmi", grm = A, data = my_data, covs = c("age", "sex"))  # 3. Many traits x models -> tidy data frame res <- herit_batch(   traits    = c("bmi", "hdl", "systolic_bp"),   grm       = A,   data      = my_data,   covs_list = list(     unadj = NULL,     cov1  = c("age", "sex"),     cov2  = c("age", "sex", "age2")   ) )  # 4. Forest plot plot_forest(res, model_filter = "cov2")

what: Ritable: Pedigree-Based Heritability Estimation for Family Cohort Studies — Ritable-package
title: Ritable: Pedigree-Based Heritability Estimation for Family Cohort Studies — Ritable-package
text: Provides profile-likelihood variance-components estimation narrow-sense heritability (h2) quantitative traits family cohort studies. Additive genetic relationship matrices built pedigrees via 'kinship2'. Phenotypes inverse-normal transformed internally. Likelihood-ratio tests use one-sided chi-squared boundary correction equivalent SOLAR Eclipse. Ninety-five percent confidence intervals derived profile likelihood rather Wald approximations. Batch estimation many traits returns tidy data frames ready downstream visualisation (forest plots, heatmaps).

title: Ritable: Pedigree-Based Heritability Estimation for Family Cohort Studies — Ritable-package
text: Maintainer: Lucas França lucas.franca@northumbria.ac.uk (ORCID) Authors: Lucas França lucas.franca@northumbria.ac.uk (ORCID) Mario Leocadio-Miguel mario.miguel@northumbria.ac.uk (ORCID)

code: Ritable_colours

what: Build an additive genetic relationship matrix from a pedigree — build_grm
title: Build an additive genetic relationship matrix from a pedigree — build_grm
text: Constructs additive genetic relationship matrix (= 2 x kinship matrix) set study subjects, using pedigree may include additional founder parents study sample.

title: Build an additive genetic relationship matrix from a pedigree — build_grm
code: build_grm(   ped_df,   study_ids = NULL,   id_col = "id",   pat_col = "pat",   mom_col = "mom",   sex_col = "sex" )

title: Build an additive genetic relationship matrix from a pedigree — build_grm
text: ped_df data frame least columns id, pat, mom, sex. Missing parents NA 0. sex numeric: 1 = male, 2 = female (value recoded 1 warning). study_ids Character integer vector IDs study subjects whose sub-matrix extracted. Defaults IDs ped_df. id_col, pat_col, mom_col, sex_col Column names four required pedigree fields. Defaults "id", "pat", "mom", "sex".

title: Build an additive genetic relationship matrix from a pedigree — build_grm
text: symmetric numeric matrix dimension length(study_ids) x length(study_ids), diagonal entries equal 1 non-inbred individuals row/column names matching study_ids.

title: Build an additive genetic relationship matrix from a pedigree — build_grm
text: function calls kinship2::kinship() full pedigree (including founders), multiplies 2 obtain additive relationship matrix, finally subsets study_ids. Including founders pedigree ensures kinship coefficients study subjects connected founders estimated correctly.

title: Build an additive genetic relationship matrix from a pedigree — build_grm
code: # Minimal two-generation pedigree: two couples, four offspring ped <- data.frame(   id  = 1:8,   pat = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 3, 3),   mom = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 2, 2, 4, 4),   sex = c(1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2) ) A <- build_grm(ped, study_ids = 5:8) round(A, 3) #>     5   6   7   8 #> 5 1.0 0.5 0.0 0.0 #> 6 0.5 1.0 0.0 0.0 #> 7 0.0 0.0 1.0 0.5 #> 8 0.0 0.0 0.5 1.0

what: Batch heritability estimation over multiple traits — herit_batch
title: Batch heritability estimation over multiple traits — herit_batch
text: convenience wrapper around herit_vc() iterates vector trait names, optionally across multiple covariate models, returns tidy data frame.

title: Batch heritability estimation over multiple traits — herit_batch
code: herit_batch(   traits,   grm,   data,   covs_list = NULL,   id_col = "IID",   min_n = 80L,   ci_level = 0.95,   .progress = TRUE )

title: Batch heritability estimation over multiple traits — herit_batch
text: traits Character vector trait column names data. grm Numeric matrix: additive genetic relationship matrix returned build_grm(). data Data frame containing ID, trait, covariate columns. covs_list named list covariate vectors, element defines one covariate model. NULL, single unadjusted model run. Example: list(unadj = NULL, cov1 = c("age", "sex"), cov2 = c("age", "sex", "age2")). id_col Name individual ID column. Default "IID". min_n Minimum sample size attempt estimation. Default 80. ci_level Profile-likelihood CI level. Default 0.95. .progress Logical. Show cli progress bar. Default TRUE.

title: Batch heritability estimation over multiple traits — herit_batch
text: data frame (tibble-compatible) one row per successfully fitted model columns: label, trait, covariates, n, h2, se, ci_lo, ci_hi, pval, sigma2_a, sigma2_e. Failed / skipped models silently omitted.

title: Batch heritability estimation over multiple traits — herit_batch
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ A    <- build_grm(my_pedigree, study_ids = my_data$IID)  res  <- herit_batch(   traits     = c("bmi", "systolic_bp", "hdl"),   grm        = A,   data       = my_data,   covs_list  = list(     unadj = NULL,     cov1  = c("age", "sex"),     cov2  = c("age", "sex", "age2")   ) )  # Significant adjusted models subset(res, grepl("cov2", label) & pval < 0.05) } # }

title: Profile-likelihood variance-components heritability estimation — herit_vc
text: Estimates narrow-sense heritability (h²) single quantitative trait using profile-likelihood variance-components approach equivalent SOLAR Eclipse. phenotype inverse-normal transformed internally.

title: Profile-likelihood variance-components heritability estimation — herit_vc
code: herit_vc(   trait,   grm,   data,   covs = NULL,   id_col = "IID",   label = NULL,   min_n = 80L,   ci_level = 0.95,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Profile-likelihood variance-components heritability estimation — herit_vc
text: trait Character string: name trait column data. grm Numeric matrix: additive genetic relationship matrix individuals data, returned build_grm(). Row column names must match ID column data. data Data frame containing id_col, trait, covariate columns. covs Character vector covariate column names, NULL intercept-model. Covariates mean-centred scaled unit variance fitting. id_col Name individual ID column data. Default "IID". label Optional string label model (used batch output). NULL, defaults "<trait>_adj" "<trait>_unadj". min_n Minimum number complete observations required attempt estimation. Models fewer observations return NULL silently. Default 80. ci_level Confidence level profile-likelihood interval. Default 0.95. verbose Logical. Print progress console. Default TRUE.

title: Profile-likelihood variance-components heritability estimation — herit_vc
text: named list elements: label Model label. trait Trait name. covariates Covariate names joined "+", "". n Sample size dropping missing values. h2 MLE narrow-sense heritability. se Standard error profile-likelihood curvature (Wald). ci_lo, ci_hi Profile-likelihood confidence interval bounds. pval One-sided LRT p-value chi-squared(1) boundary correction. sigma2_a Additive genetic variance component. sigma2_e Residual (environmental) variance component. Returns NULL n < min_n GRM subset degenerate.

title: Profile-likelihood variance-components heritability estimation — herit_vc
text: Model: Omega = sigma2_p [h2 + (1 - h2) I_n] Optimisation: eigendecomposition followed 1-D profile-likelihood optimisation h2 (0, 1) using coarse grid seed stats::optimize(). LRT: one-sided chi-squared(1) boundary correction – null boundary parameter space (h2 = 0), p-value halved relative standard chi-squared test. matches SOLAR Eclipse convention. CIs: derived uniroot() profile log-likelihood, falling back Wald interval root-finding fails.

title: Profile-likelihood variance-components heritability estimation — herit_vc
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Build GRM then estimate heritability A   <- build_grm(my_pedigree, study_ids = my_data$IID) res <- herit_vc("bmi", grm = A, data = my_data,                 covs = c("age", "sex", "age2")) str(res) } # }

title: Inverse-normal (rank-based) transformation — int_transform
text: Applies rank-based inverse-normal transformation (INT) numeric vector, placing empirical quantiles onto standard normal scale.

title: Inverse-normal (rank-based) transformation — int_transform
code: int_transform(x, ties = "average")

title: Inverse-normal (rank-based) transformation — int_transform
text: x Numeric vector. NAs preserved. ties Method passed base::rank(). Default "average".

title: Inverse-normal (rank-based) transformation — int_transform
text: numeric vector length x, NAs positions non-missing values transformed approximate normality.

title: Inverse-normal (rank-based) transformation — int_transform
text: Blom-style formula used : $$\Phi^{-1}\!\left(\frac{r_i - 0.5}{n}\right)$$ \(r_i\) rank observation \(\) \(n\) number non-missing observations. standard transformation used SOLAR Eclipse variance-components heritability software.

title: Inverse-normal (rank-based) transformation — int_transform
code: set.seed(1) x <- rexp(200, rate = 0.5)   # right-skewed hist(x,              main = "Raw")  hist(int_transform(x), main = "INT")

what: Forest plot of heritability estimates — plot_forest
title: Forest plot of heritability estimates — plot_forest
text: Produces ggplot2 forest plot output herit_batch() list herit_vc() results coerced data frame.

title: Forest plot of heritability estimates — plot_forest
code: plot_forest(   results,   model_filter = NULL,   colour_by = "trait",   sig_threshold = 0.05,   title = NULL,   x_limits = c(0, 1) )

title: Forest plot of heritability estimates — plot_forest
text: results Data frame returned herit_batch(), containing least columns label, trait, h2, ci_lo, ci_hi, pval. model_filter Optional character vector model name substrings keep (matched label). E.g. "cov2" show age + sex + age-squared model. colour_by Column colour points . Default "trait". Set NULL monochrome plot. sig_threshold Numeric. Traits pval threshold shown filled point; others open point. Default 0.05. title Optional plot title string. x_limits Numeric vector length 2 x-axis range. Default c(0, 1).

title: Forest plot of heritability estimates — plot_forest
text: ggplot2::ggplot object. Colours follow Ritable_colours palette default (pink #FE9EC7 blue #44ACFF primary pair).

title: Forest plot of heritability estimates — plot_forest
text: Requires ggplot2 (listed Suggests). informative error thrown installed.

title: Forest plot of heritability estimates — plot_forest
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ res <- herit_batch(c("bmi", "hdl", "systolic_bp"),                    grm = A, data = my_data,                    covs_list = list(unadj = NULL,                                     cov2  = c("age", "sex", "age2")))  plot_forest(res, model_filter = "cov2", title = "Adjusted heritability") } # }

title: Ritable 0.1.0 (2026-05)
text: build_grm() — build additive genetic relationship matrix pedigree data frame via kinship2::kinship(). Supports custom column names, graceful handling missing parents, informative errors common mistakes. herit_vc() — profile-likelihood variance-components heritability estimator single quantitative trait. Features: inverse-normal transformation, one-sided LRT chi-squared(1) boundary correction, profile-likelihood 95% CIs, zero-variance covariate detection. herit_batch() — iterate herit_vc() many traits x covariate model combinations; returns tidy data frame. Includes cli progress bar. int_transform() — exported rank-based inverse-normal transformation (Blom-style). plot_forest() — ggplot2 forest plot method herit_batch() output, optional model filtering significance shading.
